nativeRange.habitat: Shallow subtidal areas with sand, silt or rubble bottoms and seagrass beds; generally reported between roughly 1 and 78 m depth

diet.notes: Generally accepts live foods most readily — feeder shrimp, grass/glass shrimp, and small live fish are commonly used to initiate feeding. Some individuals have been reported to transition onto frozen meaty foods (mysis, krill, silversides) through patient stick-feeding, particularly when conditioned young. Feeding two to four times per week tends to be sufficient given the species' slow, ambush-predator metabolism. Fatty feeder fish such as goldfish are commonly considered unsuitable for long-term use.

imageCandidates.notes: Lakshmi Sawitri; 1441x1080. Tack-sharp, vibrant orange live individual, whole animal in frame with the tall serrated sailfin dorsal raised over the leaf-mimic body — the diagnostic look.
Justin Philbois; 2048x1536; CC0. Sharp tan/pink individual, complete lateral profile showing dorsal sail and fanned pectoral fin; clean dark-sand background.
Pierre Pericard; 1639x2048. Dark-brown colour phase perched among seagrass (accurate biotope), tall dorsal fin raised; shows colour-phase variation and the leaf-mimic posture.

summary: The Sailfin/Rogue Scorpion is a cryptic, leaf-mimicking ambush predator from Indo-Pacific seagrass beds and rubble flats. Its dorsal, anal, and pelvic spines carry venom, and handling with bare hands is strongly discouraged. Best kept singly in a peaceful community tank of at least 150 L.
careNotes:
The Sailfin/Rogue Scorpion, also known as the Cockatoo Waspfish, is currently accepted under the scientific name Ablabys taenianotus, though the older synonym Amblyapistus taenionotus remains widely used in the aquarium trade. It is a cryptic, slow-moving ambush predator that mimics a drifting dead leaf or piece of seagrass, often rocking gently back and forth in the bottom surge. Reported color phases include red, yellow, brown, and blue.

Venom: The dorsal, anal, and pelvic spines carry venom and have been reported to cause intense pain on contact. Handling with bare hands is strongly discouraged; hot-water immersion is the commonly cited first aid for stings.

The species is generally considered nocturnal — most active at dusk and after lights go out — which affects both the feeding schedule (target-feeding in the evening tends to be most effective) and tankmate choice, since fast daytime feeders may outcompete it for food.

Reef safety is best treated as 'with caution': any ornamental shrimp or fish small enough to fit in the mouth is at genuine risk of predation, regardless of how peaceful the tank otherwise appears.

Husbandry style: The Sailfin/Rogue Scorpion prefers slower flow, a soft sand or rubble substrate, and dense rockwork or seagrass to hide and perch among. Water quality should be kept high, as the species is reported to be sensitive to declining conditions. Only one specimen is generally recommended per tank unless a confirmed mated pair is acquired together.
